questions.prompt: An amplifier has open-loop gain A = 500 and feedback factor β = 0.01. What is the closed-loop gain A_f?
questions.explanation: A_f = A/(1+Aβ) = 500/(1+500×0.01) = 500/(1+5) = 500/6 ≈ 83.3. The desensitivity factor D = 6, so the gain is divided by 6.

questions.prompt: The loop gain T is defined as:
questions.explanation: Loop gain T = Aβ, the product of the open-loop amplifier gain and the feedback factor. It represents the gain around the entire feedback loop.

questions.prompt: The desensitivity factor D is defined as:
questions.explanation: D = 1 + Aβ. It is the factor by which the open-loop gain A is divided to get the closed-loop gain A_f = A/D.

questions.prompt: When Aβ >> 1, the closed-loop gain A_f approximately equals:
questions.explanation: When Aβ >> 1: A_f = A/(1+Aβ) ≈ A/(Aβ) = 1/β. The gain becomes independent of A and depends only on the stable feedback network β.

questions.prompt: What is the primary trade-off of applying negative feedback to an amplifier?
questions.explanation: Negative feedback reduces gain by the factor D = 1+Aβ. All other effects (reduced distortion, better impedance, wider bandwidth) are benefits. Reduced gain is the only cost.

questions.prompt: In the feedback block diagram, the error signal X_i is:
questions.explanation: For negative feedback: X_i = X_S - X_f. The feedback signal X_f is subtracted from the source signal X_S to produce the error (difference) signal that drives the amplifier.

questions.prompt: The feedback factor β in a feedback amplifier is defined as:
questions.explanation: β = X_f / X_o. It is the ratio of the feedback signal to the output signal. β is a property of the feedback network only, not the amplifier.

questions.prompt: Which of the following distinguishes transistor β (h_FE) from feedback network β?
options: h_FE is the current gain (I_C/I_B), feedback β is the ratio X_f/X_o — completely different quantities
They are both dimensionless fractions less than 1
questions.correctAnswer: h_FE is the current gain (I_C/I_B), feedback β is the ratio X_f/X_o — completely different quantities
questions.explanation: Transistor h_FE = I_C/I_B (typically 50–300). Feedback β = X_f/X_o (typically much less than 1, set by resistor ratios). Same symbol, completely different physical quantities.

questions.prompt: An amplifier uses complementary NPN/PNP transistor pairs primarily to:
questions.explanation: NPN and PNP transistors have opposite VBE temperature coefficients. Pairing them causes their temperature drifts to cancel, improving DC stability before any feedback is applied.

questions.prompt: If A = 1000 and β = 0.001, what is the desensitivity factor D?
questions.explanation: D = 1 + Aβ = 1 + 1000×0.001 = 1 + 1 = 2. The closed-loop gain A_f = 1000/2 = 500. The loop gain Aβ = 1 is modest, so feedback effects are not dramatic.

questions.prompt: What happens when the feedback signal and input signal are added together (instead of subtracted)?
options: Positive feedback — the system may oscillate
questions.correctAnswer: Positive feedback — the system may oscillate
questions.explanation: Positive feedback (X_i = X_S + X_f) causes the gain to be A/(1-Aβ). When Aβ = 1, gain → ∞ and the system oscillates. This is the basis for oscillator design.

questions.prompt: In a two-stage amplifier, if each stage has gain of -10 (inverting), the overall open-loop gain and the feedback nature are:
options: A = +100, negative feedback requires careful phase checking
questions.correctAnswer: A = +100, negative feedback requires careful phase checking
questions.explanation: (-10) × (-10) = +100. Two inverting stages give non-inverting overall gain. Whether the overall feedback is negative or positive depends on where and how the feedback is connected — the phase must be carefully checked.
